title: 5 Reasons to Switch Medicare Advantage Plans (And How to Do It Right)
rawContent:
Why Reviewing Your Medicare Advantage Plan Each Year Matters

More than 33 million Americans, roughly 51% of all Medicare-eligible beneficiaries, are currently enrolled in Medicare Advantage plans, according to Kaiser Family Foundation data. Yet only 10–11% of those enrollees actually switch plans during the Annual Enrollment Period each year. That gap matters more than most people realize.

Medicare Advantage plans can change their benefits, provider networks, drug formularies, and cost-sharing every January 1. A plan that served you well last year may look very different this year. As Tricia Neuman, Executive Director of the Program on Medicare Policy at KFF, puts it: "Medicare beneficiaries often stick with the same plan year after year even when better options are available, which can lead to higher costs and gaps in coverage they may not notice until they need care."

This article lays out a clear decision-making framework: five concrete reasons to consider switching, the enrollment windows that allow it, how to do a true cost comparison, and a step-by-step switching process, so you can make a confident, informed choice rather than defaulting to the status quo.

5 Reasons to Consider Switching Your Medicare Advantage Plan

1. Your Costs Have Increased

Plan premiums, copays, and out-of-pocket maximums can all rise from one year to the next. In 2026, the federal maximum out-of-pocket (MOOP) limit for in-network services is $9,250, but individual plans set their own caps, and those figures vary widely. Comparing the MOOP across plans, not just the monthly premium, is one of the most important financial steps you can take before deciding to switch.

2. Your Doctors or Specialists Are No Longer In-Network

Provider network changes happen quietly but can have a real impact on your care. If your primary care physician or a specialist you rely on has left your plan's network, your out-of-pocket costs could increase significantly. Always verify network status for your key providers before committing to a plan, or staying in one.

3. Your Prescriptions Are No Longer Covered or Cost More

Drug formulary tier changes are a frequent and often overlooked reason to switch. A medication that was covered at a low cost-sharing tier last year could be moved to a higher tier, or dropped entirely. Comparing prescription drug coverage across plans before enrollment ends can save you hundreds of dollars annually.

4. Your Health Needs Have Changed

A new diagnosis, upcoming surgery, or increased specialist visits can make a different plan type far more cost-effective. For example, a Medicare Advantage PPO may offer more flexibility than an HMO if you now need access to out-of-network specialists. Matching your plan type to your actual health usage, not what you needed three years ago, is smart, proactive planning.

5. Better Plan Options Are Now Available in Your Area

KFF data shows that beneficiaries have access to an average of 43 Medicare Advantage plans per county during the Annual Enrollment Period. As Juliette Cubanski, Deputy Director of the Program on Medicare Policy at KFF, notes: "Beneficiaries who take the time to compare plans annually can often find meaningful savings on premiums, drug costs, or out-of-pocket maximums." More choices means more opportunity, if you take the time to look.

When You Can Switch Medicare Advantage Plans: Enrollment Period Rules

Knowing when you're allowed to switch is just as important as knowing why. There are two primary windows and one exception-based pathway.

  • Annual Enrollment Period (AEP): October 15 – December 7 each year. This is the main window to switch Medicare Advantage plans, return to Original Medicare, or change your drug coverage. Changes take effect January 1 of the following year.

  • Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (MA OEP): January 1 – March 31 each year. During this period, you can make a one-time switch from one Medicare Advantage plan to another, or return to Original Medicare. This is a separate window from the fall AEP, and a commonly misunderstood one.

Casey Schwarz, Senior Counsel for Education and Federal Policy at the Medicare Rights Center, explains it well: "There is real confusion among beneficiaries about when they can switch Medicare Advantage plans. The Annual Enrollment Period and the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period serve different purposes, and knowing the difference can protect your coverage."

You can review the full details on Medicare Advantage enrollment periods to understand how each window applies to your situation.

To help clarify the differences between these enrollment periods, here's a quick comparison:

Enrollment Period

Dates

What You Can Do

Effective Date

Annual Enrollment Period (AEP)

October 15 – December 7

Switch Medicare Advantage plans, return to Original Medicare, join/switch/drop a Part D plan.

January 1 of the following year

Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (MA OEP)

January 1 – March 31

Make a one-time switch from one Medicare Advantage plan to another, or return to Original Medicare.

First day of the month after the plan receives your request

Special Enrollment Period (SEP)

Varies based on qualifying life event

Switch plans due to specific circumstances (e.g., moving, losing coverage, qualifying for Extra Help).

Varies, typically the first day of the month after the plan receives your request

Special Enrollment Periods: Mid-Year Switches You May Not Know About

A common question is whether you can switch Medicare Advantage plans mid-year. In most cases, the answer is no, but Special Enrollment Periods (SEPs) are the important exception. Certain qualifying life events open a time-limited window to make changes outside of the AEP and MA OEP.

Common SEP triggers include:

  • Moving out of your plan's service area

  • Your plan losing its Medicare contract

  • Qualifying for Extra Help (Low-Income Subsidy)

  • Moving into or out of a nursing facility

  • Gaining or losing other health coverage

SEPs are typically time-limited, you generally have 60 days from the qualifying event to act. Importantly, switching between Medicare Advantage plans does not trigger a late enrollment penalty, so you can make a change with confidence when your circumstances genuinely call for it. For personalized help confirming SEP eligibility, Medicare.gov and the Medicare Rights Center are reliable starting points.

How to Compare Medicare Advantage Plans Before You Switch

Each fall, your plan is required to mail you an Annual Notice of Change (ANOC) a document outlining every benefit, network, or cost-sharing change taking effect January 1. David Lipschutz, Associate Director of Policy at the Medicare Rights Center, puts it plainly: "Beneficiaries need to carefully review their plan's Annual Notice of Change every fall, insurers can change networks, formularies, and cost-sharing, making an annual plan comparison essential before the December 7 deadline."

Use this comparison checklist when evaluating plans:

  1. Monthly plan premium added on top of the standard $202.90 Part B premium in 2026

  2. In-network MOOP look for plans set well below the $9,250 federal cap

  3. Drug formulary tier placement confirm your specific medications and their cost tier

  4. Specialist and primary care network verify your current providers are included

  5. Prior authorization requirements some plans require approval for more services than others

Avoid premium-only thinking. A $0-premium plan can ultimately cost more if its copays, deductibles, and MOOP are higher. The Medicare Plan Finder at Medicare.gov lets you model total annual costs based on your specific doctors and medications. The State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP) also offers free, unbiased counseling in every state, a valuable no-cost resource for side-by-side plan comparisons.

How to Switch Medicare Advantage Plans: Step-by-Step

The actual process of switching is straightforward once you know where to start.

  1. Confirm your enrollment window. Verify that you are within the AEP (October 15 – December 7), the MA OEP (January 1 – March 31), or a qualifying SEP before taking any action.

  2. Compare plans using the Medicare Plan Finder. Go to Medicare.gov and enter your zip code, current medications, and preferred providers. Filter results by total estimated cost, not just premium.

  3. Enroll in your new plan. You can enroll through Medicare.gov, by calling 1-800-MEDICARE, or through a licensed Medicare agent. In most cases, your new plan will coordinate the disenrollment from your old plan, you do not need to formally cancel your current plan separately.

  4. Confirm your enrollment. Look for a written confirmation from your new plan, check that your member ID card arrives before your coverage start date, and verify that your prescriptions are covered under the new formulary.

Switching from Medicare Advantage Back to Original Medicare: What to Know First

During the MA OEP or the AEP, you have the right to disenroll from Medicare Advantage and return to Original Medicare. If you do, you'll also want to consider enrolling in a standalone Part D plan to maintain prescription drug coverage.

The most critical factor to understand is the Medigap risk. In most states, returning to Original Medicare does not guarantee access to a Medicare Supplement (Medigap) plan. Insurers can use medical underwriting, and may deny coverage or charge higher premiums based on your health status. This is a significant financial consideration that catches many beneficiaries off guard.

Exceptions exist: some states have guaranteed-issue Medigap protections, and certain narrow windows, such as your initial enrollment at 65 or the MA OEP, may offer a safer pathway back to supplement coverage. The process of switching from Medicare Advantage to a supplement involves timing decisions that are worth discussing with a licensed agent or SHIP counselor before acting.

Weigh the trade-offs honestly. Medicare Advantage offers extra benefits, an annual MOOP cap, and often lower premiums. Original Medicare offers broader provider access and no network restrictions. Neither is universally better, it depends on your health, finances, and priorities.

Making a Confident Decision: Your Medicare Plan Review Action Plan

The framework is straightforward: review your ANOC each fall, run a total cost comparison (not just the premium), confirm your doctors and drugs are still covered, and act before December 7. These four steps, done annually, are enough to ensure your coverage stays aligned with your actual needs.

Don't let inertia make the decision for you. With an average of 43 plan options per county and the potential for meaningful savings on premiums, drug costs, or out-of-pocket maximums, there's real value in taking an hour each fall to compare. Free help is available through the Medicare Plan Finder at Medicare.gov, your local SHIP counselor, and the Medicare Rights Center helpline.

Switching plans is a normal, protected right, not a complicated or risky process. The goal each year is simply to match your coverage to where your health and budget actually stand today, not where they were when you first enrolled. Licensed Medicare agents can also help you compare the best Medicare Advantage plans available in your area at no cost to you.

Frequently Asked Questions About Switching Medicare Advantage Plans

When can I switch my Medicare Advantage plan?

The primary window is the Annual Enrollment Period (AEP), October 15 – December 7, with changes taking effect January 1. You can also make a one-time switch during the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period (MA OEP), January 1 – March 31. Qualifying life events may also open a Special Enrollment Period for mid-year changes.

Can I switch Medicare Advantage plans mid-year?

Generally no, mid-year switches are only permitted if you qualify for a Special Enrollment Period. Common triggers include moving out of your plan's service area, your plan losing its Medicare contract, or qualifying for Extra Help. Outside of these events, you must wait for the AEP or MA OEP.

How do I switch from one Medicare Advantage plan to another?

During an eligible enrollment period, compare plans via the Medicare Plan Finder at Medicare.gov, then enroll online, by calling 1-800-MEDICARE, or through a licensed agent. Your new plan coordinates the disenrollment from your old plan in most cases, confirm your effective date and watch for your new member ID card.

What is the Medicare Advantage Open Enrollment Period?

The MA OEP runs January 1 – March 31 each year. You can switch from one Medicare Advantage plan to another, or return to Original Medicare and add a standalone Part D plan. Only one change is permitted during this window, and it is entirely separate from the fall Annual Enrollment Period.

Can I switch from Medicare Advantage back to Original Medicare?

Yes, during the AEP or MA OEP. Be aware that returning to Original Medicare does not automatically qualify you for a Medigap plan; insurers in most states can apply medical underwriting. Consulting a licensed agent or SHIP counselor before making this move is strongly recommended to protect your options.

What happens to my prescription drug coverage when I switch Medicare Advantage plans?

Most Medicare Advantage plans include Part D drug coverage, so your coverage transfers to the new plan's formulary when you switch. Before switching, verify your medications are covered at an acceptable cost tier under the new plan. If you return to Original Medicare, you'll need to enroll in a standalone Part D plan to maintain drug coverage.

What is the maximum out-of-pocket limit for Medicare Advantage plans in 2026?

The federal MOOP limit for Medicare Advantage in 2026 is $9,250 per year for in-network services (CMS, 2026). Individual plans may set their MOOP lower. Comparing this figure across plans, alongside the monthly premium, is one of the most important steps in evaluating whether to understand your Medicare out-of-pocket maximum before switching.

seoTitle: 5 Reasons to Switch Medicare Advantage Plans
seoDescription: Discover the top 5 reasons to switch Medicare Advantage plans and learn how to make the change correctly during Open Enrollment. Don't overpay for less coverage.
